feed.title: Our World in Data
feed.subtitle: Research and data to make progress against the world’s largest problems

entry.title: Do workers in richer countries work longer hours?
entry.summary: Workers in richer countries tend to work fewer hours than workers in poorer countries.

entry.title: Are we working more than ever?
entry.summary: Working hours for the average worker have decreased dramatically over the last 150 years.

entry.title: Are parents spending less time with their kids?
entry.summary: In many countries the amount of time parents spend with their kids has been increasing over the last 50 years.

entry.title: Who do we spend time with across our lifetime?
entry.summary: In adolescence we spend the most time with our parents, siblings, and friends; as we enter adulthood we spend more time with our co-workers, partners, and children; and in our later years we spend an increasing amount of time alone.

entry.title: The world’s energy problem
entry.summary: The world faces two energy problems: most of our energy production still produces greenhouse gas emissions, and hundreds of millions lack access to energy entirely.

entry.title: How do people across the world spend their time and what does this tell us about living conditions?
entry.summary: How people spend their time is similar in many ways across countries: we all sleep, work, eat, and enjoy leisure. But there are also important differences in the freedom people have to spend time on the things they value most.

entry.title: Why did renewables become so cheap so fast? And what can we do to use this global opportunity for green growth?
entry.summary: Fossil fuels dominate the global power supply because until very recently electricity from fossil fuels was the cheapest. This has changed dramatically. In most places power from new renewables is now cheaper than new fossil fuels.

entry.title: Our World in Data switches to Johns Hopkins University as our main data source for COVID-19 cases and deaths
entry.summary: To continue providing daily updates on the Coronavirus Pandemic, Our World in Data has switched from ECDC to Johns Hopkins University as our data source for confirmed cases and deaths.

entry.title: Breaking out of the Malthusian trap: How pandemics allow us to understand why our ancestors were stuck in poverty
entry.summary: For much of human history, our ancestors were trapped in an economy in which incomes were determined by the size of the population. The Industrial Revolution ended this Malthusian economy and made it possible for a country to leave abject poverty behind.

entry.title: Why do COVID-19 deaths in Sweden always appear to decrease in the last 10 days?
entry.summary: There are two ways that COVID-19 deaths can be presented over time: by the date of death, or the date on which the death is reported. The data for Sweden is shown by date of death – this means the most recent points should be treated as incomplete.
